text.title: Prompt Version Pinning Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every prompt version-pinning rollout must satisfy. Hard rules; gateway policy enforces them, not social pressure.

rule.statement: Every prompt has a stable `prompt_id` and an immutable `version` identifier. Versions are content-addressable (`v_{sha16}`) or monotonic (`v23`); once published, a version's content NEVER changes. Edits create a new version; the old version stays accessible for rollback and replay forever.

rationale.source: content-addressable identity in software supply chain; LLM-replay debugging research

rule.rationale: A mutable version is a debugging nightmare: an incident replay against `prompt v3` last week and against the same `prompt v3` today returns different results. Immutability is the property that makes the entire pinning runbook trustworthy.

example.title: Immutable lock-file entry
example.code: # prompts.lock.yaml (env: prod) classify_intent: active: v_a7b3f2c189de4501 history: - v_a7b3f2c189de4501 (rolled out 2026-05-19, current) - v_4419c2d80f8a8d2b (2026-04-12 → 2026-05-19, rolled back due to fp spike) - v_b8e91d70a0192334 (2026-03-01 → 2026-04-12)

rule.statement: The LLM gateway is the enforcement point. Every incoming call carries an explicit `prompt_id @ version`; calls without an explicit version are rejected with HTTP 400. The gateway's enforcement policy is environment-scoped (prod, staging, shadow) and version-locked.
rule.rationale: Client-side pinning is necessary but insufficient. The gateway is the only place that can guarantee no call leaks an unpinned prompt; client code can drift, but the gateway is one config file. Gateway enforcement closes the supply-chain hole.

rule.statement: Every prompt version change picks one of four rollout strategies and documents it in the PR: (a) shadow (no traffic, eval only, runs alongside live), (b) canary (5% traffic, 24h minimum hold), (c) guarded-flag (per-tenant or per-segment), (d) full (after canary holds OR for prompts with no eval risk). Default is canary; immediate-full requires written justification from ML lead.
rule.rationale: Direct full rollout of a prompt version is the single biggest source of customer-visible regressions. Canary catches most regressions within hours; shadow catches the rest before they reach customers. The discipline is the rollout strategy, not the optimism.

rule.statement: The gateway supports `prompt rollback {prompt_id}` returning to the previously-active version in < 60 seconds. Rollback is one CLI command; it produces a rollback-receipt artifact recording who, when, why, and which version was the rollback target. The rollback path is rehearsed quarterly with a game-day exercise.

rationale.source: MTTR research on LLM regressions; chaos-engineering practice

rule.rationale: A rollback that takes 30 minutes might as well not exist during a customer-impacting incident. The 60-second contract forces the gateway design to support it: in-memory lock file, no rebuild required, no deploy required, atomic flip. Game-day exercises catch the bit-rot that turns a working rollback into a broken one over months.

rule.statement: At least one prompt is under active A/B comparison every week; the comparison runs at the gateway (5/95 split or shadow), eval metrics are reported to the team channel each Friday, and the comparison closes within 4 weeks (no perpetual experiments). Comparisons without a planned close-date are blocked at the rollout gate.

rationale.source: experimentation cadence research; LLM-quality drift analysis

rule.rationale: Without a weekly review cadence, A/B experiments become wallpaper. The 4-week cap forces a decision and prevents indefinite tail-experiment drift. The Friday post is the discipline trigger.

list.item: prompts.lock.{env}.yaml — per-environment active version + history; mutable by ops CLI
prompts.lock.{env}.yaml is committed to git but managed by the ops CLI, not hand-edited
gateway-policy.yaml — per-environment enforcement config (reject unpinned, allowed versions)
